docs.title: Open First
docs.text: • INDEX.md

docs.title: Tracks
docs.text:
1. Foundations Track: chapters 01-15
  2. Advanced Track: chapters 16-30

docs.title: 1.1 Goal
docs.text: Build practical reinforcement learning expertise from core concepts to production systems.

docs.title: 1.2 RL Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. define environment and objective
  2. design reward and constraints
  3. train and evaluate policy
  4. validate safety and robustness
  5. deploy and monitor

docs.title: 1.3 Principles
docs.text:
• stable evaluation
  • reproducibility
  • safety constraints
  • operational readiness

docs.title: 02 - Markov Decision Processes and Environment Modeling
2.1 MDP Components
docs.text:
• states
  • actions
  • transition dynamics
  • reward function
  • discount factor

docs.title: 2.2 Markov Property
docs.text: Future depends on current state/action, not full history (under modeling assumptions).

docs.title: 2.3 Environment Types
docs.text: deterministic vs stochastic, episodic vs continuing.

docs.title: 2.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Customer routing MDP with queue state and service-level rewards.

docs.title: 3.1 Policy
docs.text: Mapping from states to action probabilities.

docs.title: 3.2 Return
docs.text: Discounted sum of future rewards.

docs.title: 3.3 Value Functions
docs.text:
• state value V(s)
  • action value Q(s,a)

docs.title: 3.4 Objective
docs.text: Find policy maximizing expected return.

docs.title: 4.1 Bellman Expectation Equation
docs.text: Recursive relation for value under a fixed policy.

docs.title: 4.2 Bellman Optimality Equation
docs.text: Defines optimal value and policy via maximization over actions.

docs.title: 4.3 Dynamic Programming
docs.text: Policy evaluation and policy/value iteration when model is known.

docs.title: 4.4 Practical Note
docs.text: DP is foundational but limited by full model/state-space requirements.

docs.title: 05 - Monte Carlo and Temporal Difference Learning
5.1 Monte Carlo
docs.text: Learn from complete episodes and empirical returns.

docs.title: 5.2 Temporal Difference
docs.text: Bootstrap from current value estimates at each step.

docs.title: 5.3 Bias-Variance Tradeoff
docs.text: MC: high variance, low bias. TD: lower variance, some bias.

docs.title: 5.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: TD learning updates call-routing value estimates after each decision step.

docs.title: 6.1 Q-Learning
docs.text: Off-policy TD control using greedy bootstrap target.

docs.title: 6.2 SARSA
docs.text: On-policy TD control using action actually selected by current policy.

docs.title: 6.3 Exploration Policy
docs.text: Typically epsilon-greedy in tabular settings.

docs.title: 6.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Adaptive menu optimization in small discrete-state system.

docs.title: 7.1 Core Tradeoff
docs.text: Explore uncertain actions vs exploit known high-reward actions.

docs.title: 7.2 Bandit Algorithms
docs.text:
• epsilon-greedy
  • UCB
  • Thompson sampling

docs.title: 7.3 Regret
docs.text: Cumulative performance gap vs optimal policy.

docs.title: 7.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Dynamic offer selection with contextual bandits for conversion lift.

docs.title: 8.1 Why Approximation
docs.text: Tabular methods fail in large or continuous state spaces.

docs.title: 8.2 Approaches
docs.text:
• linear approximation
  • neural approximation

docs.title: 8.3 Risks
docs.text: Instability and divergence without careful training design.

docs.title: 8.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Approximate value function for high-dimensional user-behavior state vectors.

docs.title: 9.1 Policy Optimization
docs.text: Directly optimize policy parameters via expected-return gradients.

docs.title: 9.2 REINFORCE Intuition
docs.text: Monte Carlo gradient estimator for stochastic policies.

docs.title: 9.3 Variance Reduction
docs.text: Use baselines and advantage estimates to stabilize updates.

docs.title: 9.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Policy-gradient controller for recommendation sequencing.

docs.title: 10.1 Actor and Critic Roles
docs.text: Actor updates policy; critic estimates value/advantage.

docs.title: 10.2 Benefits
docs.text: Combines policy optimization flexibility with lower-variance learning signals.

docs.title: 10.3 Stability Considerations
docs.text: Target networks, normalization, and trust-region style constraints can help.

docs.title: 10.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Actor-critic optimizes dynamic pricing decisions with continuous feedback.

docs.title: 11.1 Core Metrics
docs.text:
• average episodic return
  • success rate
  • sample efficiency
  • regret (where relevant)

docs.title: 11.2 Evaluation Protocol
docs.text: multiple seeds, confidence intervals, and fixed benchmark environments.

docs.title: 11.3 Pitfalls
docs.text: overfitting to simulator, unstable comparisons, and cherry-picked runs.

docs.title: 11.4 Reporting Rule
docs.text: Always report variance and failure cases, not only best run.

docs.title: 12.1 Environment Design
docs.text: Define state/action space, transitions, and realistic constraints.

docs.title: 12.2 Reward Design
docs.text: Align reward with true objective and avoid reward hacking shortcuts.

docs.title: 12.3 Shaping
docs.text: Use shaping carefully to speed learning without changing desired optimum.

docs.title: 12.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Reward combines resolution speed, quality, and customer satisfaction proxy.

docs.title: 13 - RL Pipelines, Experiment Tracking, and Reproducibility
13.1 Pipeline Stages
docs.text: environment setup -> training -> evaluation -> policy packaging.

docs.title: 13.2 Tracking Essentials
docs.text: seed, environment version, hyperparameters, reward definition, and metrics.

docs.title: 13.3 Reproducibility Controls
docs.text:
• multiple seed runs
  • fixed evaluation checkpoints
  • versioned environment configs

docs.title: 13.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Compare PPO variants with consistent seed/eval protocol.

docs.title: Lab 1
docs.text: Implement tabular Q-learning in gridworld and analyze convergence.

docs.title: Lab 2
docs.text: Train policy-gradient agent and compare with actor-critic baseline.

docs.title: Lab 3
docs.text: Tune exploration strategies in contextual bandit environment.

docs.title: Lab 4
docs.text: Design reward function and detect reward-hacking failure mode.

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: Call-routing RL policy balances service speed and escalation risk.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
• policy
  • value function
  • TD error
  • regret
  • exploration policy

docs.text:
1. environment definition
  2. reward function
  3. algorithm config
  4. evaluation protocol
  5. risk analysis

docs.title: Pre-Deployment Checklist
docs.text:

docs.title: 16 - Advanced Policy Optimization: PPO, TRPO, and Variants
16.1 Trust-Region Intuition
docs.text: Constrain policy updates to avoid destabilizing large parameter steps.

docs.title: 16.2 PPO
docs.text: Clipped objective balances stability and implementation simplicity.

docs.title: 16.3 TRPO
docs.text: Constrained optimization with KL trust-region formulation.

docs.title: 16.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: PPO policy stabilizes recommendation sequencing under noisy rewards.

docs.title: 17 - Value-Based Deep RL: DQN, Rainbow, and Beyond
17.1 DQN Core Ideas
docs.text:
• neural Q approximation
  • replay buffer

docs.title: 17.2 Rainbow Components
docs.text: Combines prioritized replay, distributional RL, double Q, and dueling networks.

docs.text: Overestimation bias and instability from non-stationary targets.

docs.title: 17.4 Real-Time Example

docs.title: 18 - Continuous Control: DDPG, TD3, and SAC

docs.title: 18.2 Algorithms
docs.text:
• DDPG
  • TD3 (reduced overestimation)
  • SAC (entropy-regularized exploration)

docs.title: 18.3 Practical Rule
docs.text: SAC is often strong baseline due to robustness and exploration behavior.

docs.title: 18.4 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Continuous control policy optimizes allocation in dynamic resource environment.

docs.title: 19.1 Model-Based Idea
docs.text: Learn or use transition model to plan actions before acting.

docs.title: 19.2 Benefits
docs.text: Higher sample efficiency versus pure model-free methods.

docs.title: 19.3 Risks
docs.text: Model bias can compound over planning horizons.

docs.title: 19.4 Real-Time Example

docs.title: 20 - Offline RL and Batch Policy Learning
20.1 Offline RL Setting
docs.text: Train policies from logged datasets without live exploration.

docs.title: 20.2 Core Challenge
docs.text: Distribution shift between logged behavior policy and learned target policy.

docs.title: 20.3 Methods
docs.text: Conservative value estimation and behavior-regularized approaches.

docs.title: 20.4 Real-Time Example

docs.text: Multiple agents interact in cooperative, competitive, or mixed environments.

docs.title: 21.3 Approaches
docs.text:

docs.title: 22.1 Hierarchical Structure
docs.text: High-level controller chooses skills/options; low-level controller executes actions.

docs.title: 22.2 Benefits
docs.text: Improved long-horizon planning and sample reuse.

docs.title: 22.3 Option Learning
docs.text: Discover reusable sub-policies for recurring subgoals.

docs.title: 22.4 Real-Time Example

docs.title: 23.1 Imitation Learning
docs.text: Learn policies from expert demonstrations.

docs.title: 23.2 Inverse RL
docs.text: Infer reward function from expert behavior.

docs.title: 23.3 Applications
docs.text: Useful when explicit reward design is difficult.

docs.title: 23.4 Real-Time Example

24.1 RLHF Workflow
docs.text:

  • reward model training

docs.title: 24.2 Alignment Goals
docs.text: Improve helpfulness, harmlessness, and policy compliance.

docs.title: 24.3 Risks
docs.text: Reward model hacking and alignment drift.

docs.title: 25 - Safe RL, Constrained Optimization, and Risk
25.1 Safety Motivation
docs.text: Many RL applications have hard constraints and unacceptable failure modes.

docs.title: 25.2 Methods
docs.text:
• constrained MDP approaches
  • risk-sensitive objectives
  • shielded policies

docs.title: 25.3 Evaluation
docs.text: Track constraint violations and worst-case outcomes, not only average return.

docs.title: 25.4 Real-Time Example

docs.title: 26.1 Generalization Problem
docs.text: Policies trained in one environment may fail under slight shifts.

docs.title: 26.2 Robustness Methods
docs.text:
• domain randomization
  • adversarial environment perturbations

docs.title: 26.3 Sim2Real Transfer
docs.text: Bridge simulation and real-world mismatch with calibration and staged rollout.

docs.title: 26.4 Real-Time Example

docs.title: 27.1 Distributed Architecture
docs.text: Separate actors, learners, replay storage, and evaluation services.

docs.title: 27.2 Throughput Considerations
docs.text: Balance sample collection rate, learner updates, and network overhead.

docs.title: 27.3 Reliability
docs.text: Use checkpointing, fault tolerance, and deterministic evaluation snapshots.

docs.title: 27.4 Real-Time Example

docs.title: 28 - Efficiency, Cost, and Inference Optimization in RL
28.1 Cost Drivers

docs.title: 28.2 Optimization Methods

• sample-efficient algorithms

docs.title: Reinforcement Learning for AI
docs.text: This folder contains a complete Foundations + Advanced documentation set.

docs.title: Open First
docs.text: • INDEX.md

docs.title: Tracks
docs.text:
1. Foundations Track: chapters 01-15
  2. Advanced Track: chapters 16-30

docs.title: 01 - Program Overview and Speech Analytics Lifecycle
Module Goal
docs.text: Build a production-ready understanding of speech analytics from audio capture to business actions, grounded in SLP3 (Jurafsky & Martin, 2026).

docs.title: End-to-End Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Capture and ingest: telephony, meeting, bot, and media streams.
  2. Preprocess: resample, denoise, segment with VAD, normalize loudness.
  3. Transcribe and segment speakers: ASR + diarization.
  4. Analyze: intent, sentiment, emotion, compliance, conversation quality.
  5. Act: alerts, agent assist, summaries, QA scoring, workflow triggers.
  6. Operate: monitor drift, latency, costs, model quality, and incidents.

docs.title: Architecture Building Blocks
docs.text:
• Audio gateway and stream processor
  • Feature/embedding service
  • ASR + diarization services
  • NLP analytics layer (classification, extraction, summarization)
  • Serving APIs, dashboards, and case-management integrations

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: A banking contact center streams live calls. Within 2 seconds, the system flags missing KYC statements, detects customer frustration, and recommends a compliant response script to the agent desktop.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text:
• Ch. 14: speech features and prosody foundations
  • Ch. 15: ASR pipeline and WER
  • Ch. 22: sentiment/affect lexicons
  • Ch. 25: conversation structure

docs.title: What to Track from Day 1
docs.text:
• Quality: WER, DER, intent F1, sentiment calibration
  • Reliability: p95 end-to-end latency, drop rate, retry rate
  • Governance: PII redaction success, policy violations, access audit events

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Every downstream metric in speech analytics is limited by signal quality. Bad sampling or clipping propagates into ASR, diarization, and sentiment errors.

docs.title: Sampling Rate (Practical Understanding)
docs.text:
Definition: Sampling Rate (Sr) is the number of audio samples captured per second.

Examples: - 44100 Hz -> 44.1K samples/second - 16000 Hz -> 16K samples/second

Nyquist reminder:

\[ f_{max} = \frac{Sr}{2} \]

If Sr = 16000, then the maximum captured frequency is 8000 Hz.

Practical insight: - ASR pipelines usually standardize to 16kHz. - Music/audio mastering commonly uses 44.1kHz or 48kHz. - Higher Sr means larger file size and more compute. - Lower Sr reduces size but may remove useful high-frequency detail.

Goal: keep frequencies that matter for speech understanding, not unnecessary bandwidth.

docs.title: Mono vs Stereo
docs.text:
• Mono: single channel, standard for speech tasks and ASR.
  • Stereo: two channels, common in music and immersive audio.

librosa loading behavior: - mono=True forces single channel output. - mono=False keeps original channel layout.

docs.title: Bit Depth and Amplitude Scaling
docs.text:
• Bit depth controls amplitude resolution and quantization precision.
  • librosa.load() returns float samples typically scaled in [-1, +1].

Why this helps: - Stable ML training - Consistent preprocessing across datasets

docs.title: Duration Calculation
docs.text: Use librosa.get_duration() to: - Validate recording length - Detect truncated clips - Normalize dataset assumptions (e.g., minimum call segment length)

docs.title: Practical Standards
docs.text:
• Normalize to canonical input for speech models (mono, 16kHz, 16-bit PCM).
  • Keep channel/source metadata (agent/customer, mic type, region).
  • Detect clipping, silence-only segments, and packet loss before model inference.

docs.title: Minimal Example (Librosa)
docs.text:
import librosa

y, sr = librosa.load("call.wav", sr=16000, mono=True)
duration_sec = librosa.get_duration(y=y, sr=sr)
print(sr, duration_sec, y.min(), y.max())  # 16000, duration, range near [-1, 1]

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: A telecom support team sees rising WER in one region. Root cause analysis finds mixed sampling (8kHz, 16kHz, 44.1kHz) and inconsistent channel conversion. Enforcing mono + 16kHz at ingestion stabilizes ASR quality and lowers correction effort.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text:
• Ch. 14.4 Acoustic phonetics and signals
  • Ch. 14.3 Prosody basics

docs.title: 03 - Audio Formats, Preprocessing, and Quality Control
Objective
docs.text: Design a robust preprocessing stage so ASR and analytics models receive stable, clean, and traceable inputs.

docs.title: Input Format Strategy
docs.text:
• Accept: WAV, FLAC, MP3, AAC, Opus, streaming RTP/WebRTC
  • Canonical internal representation: PCM WAV (mono, 16kHz unless use case requires otherwise)
  • Preserve source metadata for auditability and replay

docs.title: Preprocessing Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Silence trimming and VAD segmentation
  2. Loudness normalization (LUFS/RMS targets)
  3. Noise suppression and dereverberation
  4. Optional echo cancellation
  5. Channel separation for two-party calls

docs.title: Silence Trimming (Feature Engineering Step)
docs.text:
Use librosa.effects.trim() to remove: - Leading silence - Trailing silence - Very low-amplitude edge noise

Typical use cases: - Remove dead air at call start/end - Clean voice-note datasets - Improve feature stability for short utterances

docs.title: Pre-Emphasis
docs.text:
Pre-emphasis boosts higher frequencies and sharpens formant-related information.

Common transform:

\[ y[t] = x[t] - \alpha x[t-1] \]

Typical coefficient: alpha = 0.95 to 0.97.

Why it helps: - Enhances high-frequency clarity - Improves downstream speech feature quality in some pipelines

docs.title: Framing and Hop Length (Windowing)
docs.text:
Speech is non-stationary over long spans, so we analyze short windows.

Standards: - Frame length: 25 ms - Hop length: 10 ms

Why: - Speech is approximately stationary within 20-30 ms windows. - Too large a hop can miss fast acoustic events. - Too small a hop increases compute and feature volume.

docs.title: Resampling
docs.text:
Resampling aligns data with model requirements.

Example: - 44.1kHz media audio -> 16kHz ASR input

Benefits: - Consistent feature extraction - Lower inference cost - Reduced mismatches between train and serve pipelines

docs.title: Normalization (Z-Score)
docs.text:
Standardize feature scale before ML training:

\[ Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \]

Implementation note: - Guard against division by zero with a small epsilon if sigma == 0.

docs.title: Practical Example (Librosa + Numpy)
docs.text:
import librosa
import numpy as np

y, sr = librosa.load("call.wav", sr=16000, mono=True)
y_trimmed, _ = librosa.effects.trim(y, top_db=25)
y_pre = librosa.effects.preemphasis(y_trimmed, coef=0.97)

frame_length = int(0.025 * sr)  # 25 ms
hop_length = int(0.010 * sr)    # 10 ms

# Example feature to normalize (RMS here)
rms = librosa.feature.rms(y=y_pre, frame_length=frame_length, hop_length=hop_length)
rms_z = (rms - rms.mean()) / (rms.std() + 1e-8)

docs.title: Quality Control Gates
docs.text:
• Audio duration bounds
  • Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) threshold
  • Clipping percentage threshold
  • Corrupt payload and timestamp continuity checks

docs.title: 04 - Feature Extraction: MFCC, Spectrogram, and Prosody
Objective
docs.text: Convert raw speech into compact features that support ASR, speaker, emotion, and conversation models.

docs.title: FFT vs STFT
docs.text:
• FFT: frequency content of the entire signal; loses time-local context.
  • STFT: frequency content over short windows through time; standard for speech processing.

docs.title: Spectrogram Interpretation
docs.text:
• X-axis: time
  • Y-axis: frequency (Hz)
  • Color/intensity: amplitude or power

Interpretation: - Dark regions: low energy/silence - Bright regions: high energy/active speech events

docs.title: Amplitude vs Power
docs.text:
Power spectrogram is based on squared amplitude:

\[ Power = Amplitude^2 \]

Convert to decibel scale using librosa.power_to_db() for more interpretable, perception-aligned visualization.

docs.title: Mel Spectrogram
docs.text:
Humans are more sensitive to low frequencies than high frequencies, so Mel scaling better matches perception.

Key parameters: - n_mels: number of Mel bands - fmin: minimum frequency - fmax: maximum frequency

docs.title: MFCC (Core Practical Feature)
docs.text:
MFCC = Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients.

Typical speech feature stack: - 12 static cepstral coefficients - 12 delta coefficients - 12 delta-delta coefficients - 1 energy - 1 delta energy - 1 delta-delta energy

Total common representation: 39 features per frame.

docs.title: MFCC vs LFCC
docs.text: Method Scale Typical usage MFCC Mel (perceptual) Most common in speech tasks LFCC Linear frequency Alternative in some pipelines

docs.title: Delta and Delta-Delta
docs.text:
Use temporal derivatives to capture motion/change over time.

Practical effect: - Better capture of speaking dynamics - Improved recognition robustness vs static-only features

docs.title: Additional Frame-Level Features
docs.text:
• RMS energy: loudness/intensity trend (librosa.feature.rms)
  • Pitch/F0: prosody and emotion signals (librosa.piptrack)
  • Spectral centroid: perceived brightness
  • Spectral bandwidth: spread of frequencies
  • Spectral rolloff: cutoff below which most energy lies
  • Spectral flatness: tonal vs noise-like character
  • Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR): sign-change density, useful for speed/noise cues

High ZCR often indicates: - Higher-frequency/noisy content - Fast or unvoiced segments

Low ZCR often indicates: - Slower voiced segments - Longer pauses and stable tones

docs.title: Voice Activity Detection (VAD)
docs.text: Separating silent and non-silent intervals: - Reduces blank audio processing - Improves feature quality - Supports downstream segmentation quality

docs.title: Practical Example (Librosa)
docs.text:
import librosa
import numpy as np

y, sr = librosa.load("call.wav", sr=16000, mono=True)

# STFT and power/dB spectrogram
S = np.abs(librosa.stft(y, n_fft=512, hop_length=160, win_length=400)) ** 2
S_db = librosa.power_to_db(S, ref=np.max)

# Mel and MFCC
mel = librosa.feature.melspectrogram(S=S, sr=sr, n_mels=80, fmin=50, fmax=7600)
mfcc = librosa.feature.mfcc(S=librosa.power_to_db(mel), n_mfcc=13)
delta = librosa.feature.delta(mfcc)
delta2 = librosa.feature.delta(mfcc, order=2)

# Extra features
rms = librosa.feature.rms(y=y)
zcr = librosa.feature.zero_crossing_rate(y)
centroid = librosa.feature.spectral_centroid(y=y, sr=sr)
bandwidth = librosa.feature.spectral_bandwidth(y=y, sr=sr)
rolloff = librosa.feature.spectral_rolloff(y=y, sr=sr)
flatness = librosa.feature.spectral_flatness(y=y)
pitch, mag = librosa.piptrack(y=y, sr=sr)

docs.title: Engineering Guidelines
docs.text:
• Use fixed frame/hop across training and inference.
  • Version feature extractors like models.
  • Track domain shifts (e.g., headset vs speakerphone frequency profile).

docs.title: Complete Feature Engineering Pipeline
docs.text: Raw audio -> Mono conversion -> Silence trimming -> Pre-emphasis -> Framing (25 ms) -> Hop (10 ms) -> STFT -> Power spectrogram -> Mel spectrogram -> MFCC/LFCC -> Delta + delta-delta -> Energy + ZCR + spectral features -> Normalization -> Model-ready feature set

docs.text:

  • Fine-tuning on in-domain audio/transcripts
  • Post-ASR normalization (numbers, acronyms, dates)
  • Human-in-the-loop correction for high-risk workflows

docs.title: 06 - Speaker Diarization and Speaker ID Basics
Objective
docs.text: Separate “who spoke when” and connect speech turns to speaker identities for analytics, QA, and compliance.

docs.title: Core Tasks
docs.text:
• Diarization: partition audio into speaker-homogeneous segments.
  • Speaker identification: map voice to known identity set.
  • Speaker verification: confirm claimed identity.

docs.title: Typical Pipeline
docs.text: • VAD -> embedding extraction (x-vector/ECAPA style) -> clustering -> overlap handling -> resegmentation

docs.title: Operational Concerns
docs.text:
• Channel mismatch can hurt speaker embeddings.
  • Overlapping speech inflates diarization error.
  • Identity systems require strong consent and governance.

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: A BPO QA platform uses diarization to isolate agent vs customer turns, enabling separate sentiment trajectories and accurate script-adherence scoring.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Detect critical phrases and classify intent quickly enough to trigger real-time actions.

docs.title: Two Complementary Tasks
docs.text:
• Keyword spotting (KWS): phrase-level trigger detection (e.g., cancellation, legal risk).
  • Intent detection: classify user goal from utterance or turn window.

docs.title: Design Patterns
docs.text:
• Fast KWS model for immediate alerts
  • Contextual intent model on rolling transcript window
  • Confidence thresholds + fallback routing logic

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Calibrate thresholds to reduce false alarms.
  • Use confusion matrices by business intent.
  • Include abstain/unknown class for out-of-domain utterances.

docs.title: Real-Time Example

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Use the right metric per component, and avoid misleading “single score” reporting.

docs.title: Component-Wise Metrics
docs.text:
• ASR: WER, CER, domain-specific keyword recall
  • Diarization: DER and overlap-aware DER
  • Intent/Compliance classifiers: precision, recall, F1, AUPRC
  • Sentiment/Emotion: macro F1, calibration error, subgroup parity gaps
  • Summarization: task-grounded human eval + factuality checks

docs.title: Evaluation Strategy
docs.text:
• Split by channel, accent, region, and domain
  • Report confidence intervals and significance when comparing models
  • Track online-vs-offline performance drift

docs.title: Data Design Principles
docs.text:

docs.text:

docs.title: Essential Glossary
docs.text:
• WER: Word Error Rate for ASR quality
  • DER: Diarization Error Rate for speaker segmentation
  • VAD: Voice Activity Detection for speech/non-speech segmentation
  • CTC: alignment-free sequence objective in ASR
  • RTF: Real-Time Factor, processing speed vs audio duration

docs.title: Objective

docs.text:

docs.title: Practical Patterns
docs.text:
• Multi-stage pipeline: LID -> multilingual ASR -> language-aware NLP
  • Shared multilingual embeddings with locale-specific adaptation
  • Separate evaluation slices by language pair and switch frequency

  • Microphone distortion and clipping

docs.title: Robustness Toolkit
docs.text:
• Neural denoising and dereverberation
  • Echo cancellation for speaker playback loops
  • Augmentation during training (noise/reverb mixes)
  • Confidence-aware downstream gating

docs.title: 25 - Fairness, Bias, and Responsible Speech AI
Objective
docs.text: Prevent performance and decision disparities across accents, dialects, language backgrounds, and demographic groups.

docs.title: Bias Risk Areas
docs.text:
• ASR error inflation by accent/dialect
  • Emotion misclassification across speaking styles
  • Uneven false positives in compliance triggers

docs.title: Mitigation Controls
docs.text:
• Slice-based evaluation and public scorecards
  • Balanced data curation and targeted augmentation
  • Threshold tuning by harm model, not only aggregate F1
  • Human appeal and override paths

docs.title: 26 - Privacy, Security, and Compliance in Speech Data
Objective
docs.text: Protect sensitive voice and transcript data across collection, storage, processing, and sharing.

docs.title: Security Baseline
docs.text:
• Encryption in transit and at rest
  • Role-based access and least privilege
  • Key management and secret rotation
  • Immutable audit logging

docs.title: Privacy Controls
docs.text:
• PII/PCI redaction in transcripts and audio snippets
  • Data minimization and retention windows
  • Consent tracking and jurisdiction-aware policy routing

docs.title: Compliance Workflow
docs.text:
• Control mapping to internal/legal policies
  • Automated evidence collection for audits
  • Continuous control testing in CI/CD

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: A healthcare call platform redacts PHI entities in near real time and blocks unauthorized playback requests via policy-based access checks.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text: • Ch. 15 and Ch. 25 outputs as sensitive operational data requiring governance

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Operate speech analytics models across environments with reliable delivery, observability, and rollback.

docs.title: MLOps Foundations
docs.text:
• CI/CD for model + feature + policy artifacts
  • Staged promotion (dev -> staging -> prod)
  • Canary and champion/challenger evaluation
  • Automated rollback rules tied to SLO breaches

docs.title: Platform Considerations
docs.text:
• GPU/CPU mix and autoscaling
  • Feature stores for reusable speech/text features
  • Registry for model and decoder versions
  • Workflow orchestration for retraining and evaluation

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: A platform rollout uses canary traffic on one call queue; rising DER triggers automatic rollback before full deployment.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text:
• Ch. 15 model changes directly affect WER and downstream KPIs
  • Ch. 4 disciplined evaluation in deployment loops

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Optimize speech analytics for business efficiency without sacrificing critical quality.

docs.title: Optimization Levers
docs.text:
• Model distillation and quantization
  • Dynamic batching for non-interactive workloads
  • Tiered inference (fast model first, heavy model on demand)
  • Cache repeated context and retrieval outputs

docs.title: Cost Engineering
docs.text:
• Separate real-time and batch pipelines
  • Track cost per audio minute and per resolved case
  • Enforce budget-aware routing policies

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: A support operation runs lightweight streaming ASR for live hints, then batch reprocesses only high-value calls with a larger model for compliance-grade transcripts.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text:
• Ch. 15 architecture choices and WER/latency tradeoff
  • Ch. 11 retrieval cost/performance balance

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Detect quality degradation early and restore service safely through repeatable incident workflows.

docs.title: Monitoring Layers
docs.text:
• Data drift: language mix, noise profile, channel changes
  • Model drift: WER/DER/F1 deterioration over time
  • System health: queue lag, p95 latency, error rate
  • Business impact: missed compliance events, false escalations

docs.title: Incident Playbook
docs.text:
1. Detect and classify severity
  2. Contain (traffic shift, model fallback)
  3. Diagnose (slice-level and pipeline-level)
  4. Recover (rollback or hotfix)
  5. Postmortem and prevention tasks

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: After a telephony codec change, WER spikes in one region. Automated monitors trigger rollback to prior front-end preprocessing profile within minutes.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text:
• Ch. 15 WER as leading ASR health signal
  • Ch. 25 conversation-level KPI monitoring implications

docs.title: 30 - Advanced Capstone: Speech Analytics System Design
Capstone Objective
docs.text: Design an enterprise speech analytics platform that is accurate, low-latency, governed, and operationally resilient.

docs.title: Required Deliverables
docs.text:
• System architecture with online and batch dataflows
  • Model suite specification (ASR, diarization, sentiment, compliance, summarization)
  • Evaluation plan with slice-level metrics and acceptance gates
  • Security/privacy and policy-governance controls
  • Deployment strategy with rollback and incident response
  • Cost model and scaling assumptions

docs.title: Minimum Success Criteria
docs.text:
• Meets defined p95 latency for live assist
  • Meets WER/DER and compliance-detection thresholds
  • Demonstrates bias and privacy control evidence
  • Includes reproducibility and monitoring design

docs.title: Real-Time Example
docs.text: Design a multinational support platform that handles code-switched calls, provides live compliance prompts, and produces audit-ready summaries with cited policy grounding.

docs.title: SLP3 Coverage Mapping
docs.text:
• Ch. 14-16 for speech modeling foundations
  • Ch. 22 for affect analytics
  • Ch. 25 for conversation structure
  • Ch. ⅞/11 for LLM and retrieval augmentation

docs.title: Speech Analytics for AI - Index
docs.text: This track is mapped to the SLP3 draft (Jurafsky & Martin, 2026), especially: - Ch. 14 Phonetics and Speech Feature Extraction - Ch. 15 Automatic Speech Recognition - Ch. 16 Text-to-Speech - Ch. 22 Lexicons for Sentiment, Affect, and Connotation - Ch. 25 Conversation and its Structure

docs.title: Speech Analytics for AI
docs.text: This track is now aligned to Speech and Language Processing (3rd ed. draft, Jan 6, 2026) with practical production workflows.

docs.title: Coverage Promise
docs.text:
• Foundations (01-15): audio, features, ASR, diarization, intent, affect, evaluation, and reproducible pipelines
  • Advanced (16-30): advanced ASR, multilingual systems, streaming architectures, LLM/RAG integration, governance, MLOps, and capstone design
  • Every module includes at least one Real-Time Example to make implementation concrete

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text: • Speech Analytics Index

docs.title: 1.1 Goal
docs.text: Build statistical intuition and practical skill to design, evaluate, and trust AI systems.

docs.title: 1.2 Why Statistics Matters in AI
docs.text:
• Models learn patterns under uncertainty
  • Metrics and validation are statistical decisions
  • Deployment quality depends on distributional assumptions

docs.title: 1.3 Learning Path
docs.text:
1. Probability and random variables
  2. Estimation and testing
  3. Bayesian updates and decision making
  4. Model diagnostics and experimental design
  5. Robustness and causal reasoning

docs.title: 1.4 Required Math Basics
docs.text:
• Algebra and functions
  • Basic calculus intuition (rates, areas)
  • Matrix basics (vectors, dot product)

docs.title: 1.5 Notation Quick Reference
docs.text:
• P(A) probability of event A
  • E[X] expectation
  • Var(X) variance
  • Cov(X, Y) covariance
  • N(mu, sigma^2) normal distribution

docs.title: 1.6 AI-Relevant Statistical Questions
docs.text:
• Is this model improvement real or noise?
  • Is this feature truly predictive or leakage?
  • Is metric drop due to drift or random fluctuation?

docs.title: 1.7 Baseline Reproducibility Rules
docs.text:
• Fix random seeds
  • Log data and model versions
  • Report uncertainty with every key metric

docs.title: 2.1 Probability Axioms
docs.text:
1. P(A) >= 0
  2. P(S) = 1
  3. For disjoint events, P(A U B) = P(A) + P(B)

docs.title: 2.2 Event Operations
docs.text:
• Union A U B
  • Intersection A ∩ B
  • Complement A^c

docs.title: 2.3 Conditional Probability
docs.text: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B) for P(B) > 0.

docs.title: 2.4 Independence
docs.text: A and B are independent if P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B).

docs.title: 2.5 Bayes Theorem
docs.text:
P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A) / P(B)

Used in AI for belief updates as new evidence arrives.

docs.title: 2.6 Total Probability
docs.text: P(B) = sum_i P(B|A_i)P(A_i) over partition {A_i}.

docs.title: 2.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Fraud detection: - Prior fraud rate low - Alert model has false positives - Bayes update is required to interpret alert probability correctly

docs.title: 2.8 Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A)
  • Ignoring base rates
  • Assuming correlation implies causal dependence

docs.title: 3.1 Random Variables
docs.text:
• Discrete: countable outcomes
  • Continuous: uncountable outcomes

docs.title: 3.2 Distribution Functions
docs.text:
• PMF for discrete RVs
  • PDF for continuous RVs
  • CDF for both

docs.title: 3.3 Expectation and Variance
docs.text:
• E[X] average long-run value
  • Var(X) spread around mean

docs.title: 3.4 Important Discrete Distributions
docs.text:
• Bernoulli: binary outcomes
  • Binomial: number of successes in n trials
  • Poisson: count of events in interval
  • Geometric: trials until first success

docs.title: 3.5 Important Continuous Distributions
docs.text:
• Uniform
  • Normal
  • Exponential
  • Log-normal

docs.title: 3.6 Joint and Marginal Distributions
docs.text: Understand how variables interact and how to isolate one variable from a joint model.

docs.title: 3.7 AI Use Cases
docs.text:
• Poisson for event counts
  • Normal approximations for errors
  • Bernoulli/Binomial for click and conversion modeling

docs.title: 4.1 Central Tendency
docs.text:
• Mean
  • Median
  • Mode

docs.title: 4.2 Dispersion
docs.text:
• Range
  • Variance
  • Standard deviation
  • Interquartile range

docs.title: 4.3 Shape and Outliers
docs.text:
• Skewness intuition
  • Heavy-tailed behavior
  • Outlier detection basics

docs.title: 4.4 Covariance and Correlation
docs.text:
• Covariance direction of joint movement
  • Correlation standardized strength of linear relation

docs.title: 4.5 Data Summaries for AI
docs.text: Always compute segment-wise stats (by class, geography, device, etc.) before modeling.

docs.title: 4.6 Visualization Essentials
docs.text:
• Histogram
  • Box plot
  • Scatter plot
  • Correlation heatmap

docs.title: 4.7 Practical Example
docs.text: For model input quality checks: 1. Summarize null percentages 2. Compare train vs validation distributions 3. Flag unstable features

docs.title: 5.1 Sampling Methods
docs.text:
• Simple random sampling
  • Stratified sampling
  • Cluster sampling
  • Convenience sampling (high risk of bias)

docs.title: 5.2 Law of Large Numbers
docs.text: Sample averages converge toward population mean as sample size grows.

docs.title: 5.3 Central Limit Theorem
docs.text: Under broad conditions, sample mean distribution approaches normal for large n.

docs.title: 5.4 Standard Error
docs.text: Quantifies uncertainty in sample estimate.

docs.title: 5.5 Point Estimation Properties
docs.text:
• Unbiasedness
  • Consistency
  • Efficiency

docs.title: 5.6 Bias-Variance Tradeoff
docs.text: Lower bias often increases variance and vice versa. Good estimators balance both.

docs.title: 5.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: You estimate model accuracy from 500 sampled predictions. CLT enables an approximate confidence interval around measured accuracy.

docs.title: 6.1 Confidence Intervals
docs.text:
A confidence interval gives a plausible range for a population parameter.

Interpretation: 95% CI does not mean a 95% chance true parameter is inside after seeing data; it refers to long-run procedure coverage.

docs.title: 6.2 Hypothesis Testing Workflow
docs.text:
1. State null and alternative hypotheses
  2. Choose test statistic
  3. Compute p-value
  4. Compare with significance level alpha
  5. Make decision and report effect size

docs.title: 6.3 Type I and Type II Errors
docs.text:
• Type I: false positive
  • Type II: false negative

docs.title: 6.4 Statistical Power
docs.text: Probability of detecting true effect when it exists.

docs.title: 6.5 Common Tests
docs.text:
• z-test, t-test
  • chi-square test
  • proportion tests
  • nonparametric alternatives

docs.title: 6.6 p-Value Misuse Warnings
docs.text:
• Not probability null is true
  • Not effect size
  • Not practical significance

docs.title: 6.7 AI Example
docs.text: Compare old vs new model conversion uplift with two-proportion z-test and report CI for uplift.

docs.title: 7.1 Bayesian Core Equation
docs.text: Posterior is proportional to prior times likelihood: P(theta|data) proportional to P(data|theta) * P(theta)

docs.title: 7.2 Components
docs.text:
• Prior: belief before seeing data
  • Likelihood: data generation model
  • Posterior: updated belief
  • Posterior predictive: prediction with uncertainty

docs.title: 7.3 MAP vs MLE
docs.text:
• MLE maximizes likelihood
  • MAP maximizes posterior (includes prior)

docs.title: 7.4 Conjugate Prior Intuition
docs.text: Some prior-likelihood pairs yield closed-form posteriors, enabling fast updates.

docs.title: 7.5 Bayesian Decision Making
docs.text: Use expected utility or expected loss for action selection.

docs.title: 7.6 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Spam probability update after observing words in an email stream using prior spam rate and likelihood ratios.

docs.title: 7.7 Practical Benefits in AI
docs.text:
• Better uncertainty handling in low-data settings
  • Natural incorporation of prior domain knowledge
  • Continuous online updates

docs.title: 8.1 Linear Regression Refresher
docs.text: Model: y = beta0 + beta1*x1 + ... + betak*xk + error

docs.title: 8.2 OLS Assumptions (Practical)
docs.text:
• Linear relationship (or transformed to near-linear)
  • Independent errors
  • Constant variance (homoscedasticity)
  • Limited multicollinearity

docs.title: 8.3 Coefficient Interpretation
docs.text: Coefficient indicates expected change in y for one unit x increase, holding others fixed.

docs.title: 8.4 Residual Diagnostics
docs.text:
• Residual plots
  • QQ plot intuition
  • Outlier and leverage checks

docs.title: 8.5 Regularization Statistics
docs.text:
• Ridge reduces variance
  • Lasso supports sparsity
  • Elastic net combines both

docs.title: 8.6 Generalization
docs.text: Focus on out-of-sample performance, not only training fit.

docs.title: 8.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Predict delivery delay from traffic/weather features and compare OLS vs Ridge on validation RMSE.

docs.title: 9.1 Confusion Matrix
docs.text:
• True Positive
  • False Positive
  • True Negative
  • False Negative

docs.title: 9.2 Metric Definitions
docs.text:
• Accuracy
  • Precision
  • Recall
  • F1 score
  • Specificity

docs.title: 9.3 Threshold Tuning
docs.text:
Predicted probability must be converted to class using threshold.

Lower threshold: - more positives found - more false positives

Higher threshold: - fewer false positives - more missed positives

docs.title: 9.4 ROC and PR Curves
docs.text:
• ROC useful for balanced classes
  • PR more informative for rare positive classes

docs.title: 9.5 Calibration
docs.text: Probability outputs should match observed frequencies.

docs.title: 9.6 Cost-Sensitive Decisions
docs.text: Choose threshold based on business cost matrix, not metric alone.

docs.title: 9.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Fraud model threshold moved from 0.5 to 0.3 to reduce missed fraud, with controlled review capacity.

docs.title: 10.1 Why Experiments
docs.text: Experiments isolate causal effect of changes (model, UI, policy).

docs.title: 10.2 Core Design Principles
docs.text:
• Random assignment
  • Control and treatment groups
  • Pre-defined success metric
  • Fixed analysis plan

docs.title: 10.3 Sample Size Planning
docs.text: Inputs: - baseline rate - minimum detectable effect - alpha - power

docs.title: 10.4 Effect Size and Practical Significance
docs.text: Statistical significance alone is insufficient; evaluate business impact.

docs.title: 10.5 Multiple Testing
docs.text: Running many tests inflates false positives. Use corrections or false discovery controls.

docs.title: 10.6 Sequential Testing Caution
docs.text: Peeking repeatedly without correction raises error rates.

docs.title: 10.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Compare recommendation model A vs B for click-through rate with pre-registered stopping rule.

docs.title: 11 - Time Series and Sequential Data Statistics
11.1 Components
docs.text:
• Trend
  • Seasonality
  • Residual noise

docs.title: 11.2 Stationarity
docs.text: Many statistical methods assume stable mean/variance over time.

docs.title: 11.3 Autocorrelation
docs.text: Past values can influence future values; quantify using ACF/PACF intuition.

docs.title: 11.4 Rolling Statistics
docs.text: Use moving averages and rolling variance for monitoring drift and volatility.

docs.title: 11.5 Forecast Evaluation
docs.text:
• MAE
  • RMSE
  • MAPE (careful with near-zero values)

docs.title: 11.6 Data Leakage in Time Series
docs.text: Never use future information during feature construction.

docs.title: 11.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Forecast daily support ticket volume and set staffing alerts with prediction intervals.

docs.title: 12.1 Correlation Is Not Causation
docs.text: Observed association can be driven by confounders.

docs.title: 12.2 Counterfactual Question
docs.text: What would have happened to same unit without treatment?

docs.title: 12.3 Confounding and Bias
docs.text:
• Omitted variables
  • Selection bias
  • Survivorship bias

docs.title: 12.4 DAG Intuition
docs.text: Directed acyclic graphs help identify adjustment sets and blocked paths.

docs.title: 12.5 Causal Strategies (Intro)
docs.text:
• Randomized experiments
  • Matching and stratification
  • Difference-in-differences intuition
  • Instrumental variable intuition

docs.title: 12.6 Simpson’s Paradox
docs.text: Global trend may reverse within subgroups. Always inspect stratified analysis.

docs.title: 12.7 AI Example
docs.text: Ad campaign appears to improve conversion overall, but subgroup analysis shows effect driven by traffic source mix shift.

docs.title: 13.1 Common Statistical Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Data leakage
  • p-hacking
  • target leakage
  • overfitting to validation set
  • cherry-picked metrics

docs.title: 13.2 Robustness Practices
docs.text:
• Cross-validation
  • Sensitivity analysis
  • Stress testing on edge cohorts
  • Repeated runs with uncertainty intervals

docs.title: 13.3 Distribution Shift
docs.text: Monitor covariate shift, label shift, and concept drift post-deployment.

docs.title: 13.4 Fairness and Bias
docs.text: Evaluate model performance across demographic and operational groups.

docs.title: 13.5 Ethics and Governance
docs.text:
• Transparent metric reporting
  • Explain assumptions and limitations
  • Escalate high-impact decisions to human oversight

docs.title: 13.6 Reliability Reporting Standard
docs.text: Every model report should include: - metric point estimate - uncertainty interval - known failure modes - data coverage limits

docs.title: Lab 1 - Probability and Bayes in AI Alerts
docs.text: Build a notebook that: 1. Simulates rare event detection 2. Computes base-rate adjusted posterior alert probability 3. Compares naive vs Bayes-calibrated interpretation

docs.title: Lab 2 - Confidence Intervals on Model Metrics
docs.text: Given classification outputs: - estimate accuracy and F1 - compute bootstrap confidence intervals - report uncertainty-aware conclusion

docs.title: Lab 3 - Hypothesis Test for Model Upgrade
docs.text:
Test whether new model improves conversion or reduces error rate.

Required output: - null/alternative - p-value - confidence interval - practical effect recommendation

docs.title: Lab 4 - A/B Test Analysis
docs.text: Analyze treatment/control outcomes with guardrails: - sample ratio mismatch check - primary and secondary metric review - stopping rule compliance check

docs.title: Lab 5 - Time-Series Drift Monitoring
docs.text: Build rolling dashboard for: - mean prediction score - error rate by week - drift alerts

docs.title: Real-Time Example 1: Support Priority Confidence
docs.text:
Input stream: 

Ticket classifier predicts P1 with probability 0.62

Decision pattern: - if calibrated probability > business threshold, auto-escalate - else route for manual review - log confidence interval trend weekly

docs.title: Real-Time Example 2: Fraud Threshold Adjustment
docs.text:
Scenario: - false negatives are expensive - investigation capacity limited

Task: - simulate thresholds from 0.1 to 0.9 - compute precision, recall, and expected cost - select threshold minimizing expected loss

docs.title: Capstone Project
docs.text: Choose one domain (support, fraud, marketing, healthcare ops) and deliver: 1. statistical problem framing 2. data summary + assumptions 3. test/estimation workflow 4. uncertainty-aware recommendations 5. reproducible report

docs.title: 15.1 Glossary
docs.text:
• Estimator: rule to estimate parameter from data
  • Standard error: uncertainty of estimator across samples
  • Confidence interval: plausible range from repeated-sampling procedure
  • p-value: extremeness probability under null model
  • Power: probability of detecting true effect
  • Calibration: probability predictions matching empirical frequency
  • Drift: distributional change over time
  • Confounder: variable affecting both treatment and outcome

docs.title: 15.2 Statistical Report Template
docs.text:
Objective
Data description
Assumptions
Method
Results (point estimate + uncertainty)
Sensitivity checks
Recommendation
Limitations

docs.title: 15.3 Model Metric Reporting Template
docs.text:
Metric: {{name}}
Point estimate: {{value}}
95% CI: {{lower}} to {{upper}}
Segment breakdown: {{segments}}
Trend vs baseline: {{delta}}

docs.title: 15.4 Pre-Deployment Statistical Checklist
docs.text:
• Data sampling method documented
  • Leakage checks passed
  • Confidence intervals reported
  • Threshold selected by cost and risk
  • Drift monitors configured
  • Rollback criteria defined

docs.title: 15.5 Post-Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
• Weekly metric uncertainty review
  • Segment-wise fairness checks
  • Drift alerts operational
  • Incident root-cause analysis template ready

docs.title: 15.6 30-Day Revision Plan
docs.text:
1. Week 1: probability + random variables
  2. Week 2: estimation + hypothesis testing
  3. Week 3: Bayesian + regression + metrics
  4. Week 4: experiments + causality + robustness labs

docs.title: 16.1 Random Vectors and Covariance Structure
docs.text:
For a random vector X in R^d: - Mean vector: mu = E[X] - Covariance matrix: Sigma = E[(X-mu)(X-mu)^T]

Sigma is symmetric and positive semi-definite. Its structure drives feature dependence modeling.

docs.title: 16.2 Multivariate Normal Distribution
docs.text:
X ~ N(mu, Sigma) is central to many AI methods.

Key properties: - Marginals and conditionals are Gaussian - Linear transforms preserve Gaussianity - Mahalanobis distance defines ellipsoidal contours

docs.title: 16.3 Covariance and Precision Matrices
docs.text:
• Covariance matrix captures pairwise dependence
  • Precision matrix Omega = Sigma^{-1} encodes conditional independence patterns (Gaussian setting)

docs.title: 16.4 Eigenvalues, SVD, and PCA
docs.text:
• Eigendecomposition and SVD are core matrix tools
  • PCA projects to low-dimensional subspace maximizing variance
  • Explained-variance analysis guides dimensionality selection

docs.title: 16.5 Whitening and Feature Decorrelation
docs.text: Whitening transforms reduce feature correlation and can stabilize optimization in some pipelines.

docs.title: 16.6 High-Dimensional Covariance Estimation
docs.text: When d approaches or exceeds n, sample covariance is unstable. Use: - Shrinkage estimators - Factor models - Sparse precision estimation

docs.title: 16.7 Real-Time AI Example
docs.text: Embedding monitoring: 1. Track mean vector and covariance of embedding batches 2. Measure drift with Mahalanobis distance 3. Alert when drift exceeds calibrated threshold

docs.title: 16.8 Practical Checklist
docs.text:
• Standardize features before covariance analysis
  • Check condition number of covariance matrix
  • Use robust alternatives when outliers are present

docs.title: 17 - Exponential Family, GLM, and Probabilistic Modeling
17.1 Exponential Family Form
docs.text:
Many common distributions can be written as: p(y|theta) = h(y) exp(theta^T T(y) - A(theta))

This unifies estimation and inference across models.

docs.title: 17.2 Sufficient Statistics
docs.text: T(y) summarizes information needed for parameter inference, reducing complexity.

docs.title: 17.3 Generalized Linear Models (GLMs)
docs.text:
GLMs connect predictors to non-Gaussian outcomes.

Components: - Random component (distribution family) - Linear predictor eta = X beta - Link function g(E[y]) = eta

docs.title: 17.4 Common GLMs in AI
docs.text:
• Logistic regression for binary outcomes
  • Poisson regression for counts
  • Gamma regression for positive skewed targets

docs.title: 17.5 Estimation and Diagnostics
docs.text:
• MLE via iterative optimization (IRLS for many GLMs)
  • Residual diagnostics and deviance
  • Overdispersion checks in count models

docs.title: 17.6 Regularized GLMs
docs.text: Use L1/L2 penalties to reduce variance and improve generalization.

docs.title: 17.7 Probabilistic Modeling Perspective
docs.text: GLMs are probabilistic prediction systems, not only curve-fitting methods. They provide uncertainty-aware outputs.

docs.title: 17.8 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Demand forecasting for ticket arrivals: - Model hourly counts via Poisson/Negative Binomial - Include calendar and event features - Output confidence bounds for staffing decisions

docs.title: 18 - Advanced Estimation: MLE, MAP, and EM
18.1 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
docs.text:
MLE chooses parameter theta maximizing likelihood of observed data.

Properties (under regularity): - Consistent - Asymptotically normal - Often efficient

docs.title: 18.2 Score and Fisher Information
docs.text:
• Score: gradient of log-likelihood
  • Fisher information: expected curvature of log-likelihood

These quantify identifiability and estimator uncertainty.

docs.title: 18.3 MAP Estimation
docs.text:
MAP adds prior information: theta_MAP = argmax log p(data|theta) + log p(theta)

Useful under data sparsity or when domain priors are strong.

docs.title: 18.4 Expectation-Maximization (EM)
docs.text:
EM handles latent variables/incomplete data.

Iteration: 1. E-step: compute expected complete-data log-likelihood 2. M-step: maximize expectation w.r.t. parameters

docs.title: 18.5 EM Use Cases in AI
docs.text:
• Gaussian Mixture Models
  • Missing-data imputation workflows
  • Hidden-variable models

docs.title: 18.6 Identifiability and Local Optima
docs.text: EM and likelihood methods can converge to local maxima. Use multi-start initialization and stability checks.

docs.title: 18.7 Uncertainty Estimation
docs.text:
• Hessian-based approximation
  • Bootstrap confidence intervals
  • Bayesian posterior alternatives

docs.title: 18.8 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Customer segmentation with GMM: - Fit with EM - Validate cluster stability across seeds - Reject unstable segment solution before deployment

docs.title: 19.1 Why Resampling
docs.text: Resampling quantifies uncertainty when analytic formulas are hard or assumptions are weak.

docs.title: 19.2 Bootstrap
docs.text:
Procedure: 1. Resample with replacement from dataset 2. Recompute statistic many times 3. Use empirical distribution for intervals/SE

Common intervals: - Percentile interval - BCa interval (bias-corrected and accelerated)

docs.title: 19.3 Jackknife
docs.text: Leave-one-out resampling for bias/variance approximation.

docs.title: 19.4 Permutation Tests
docs.text: Assess significance by shuffling labels under null hypothesis. Useful for robust nonparametric hypothesis testing.

docs.title: 19.5 Rank-Based Tests
docs.text:
• Mann-Whitney U
  • Wilcoxon signed-rank
  • Kruskal-Wallis

Useful when normality assumptions are unreliable.

docs.title: 19.6 Cross-Validation as Statistical Estimator
docs.text: CV estimates generalization error with variance; report mean and spread.

docs.title: 19.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Evaluate F1 uplift of a new classifier with bootstrap CIs: - If CI excludes zero uplift threshold, promote candidate model

docs.title: 19.8 Best Practices
docs.text:
• Preserve grouping/time structure in resampling
  • Use stratified resampling for imbalanced classes
  • Report number of resamples and random seed

docs.title: 20 - Advanced Bayesian Inference: MCMC and VI
20.1 Bayesian Inference at Scale
docs.text: Closed-form posteriors are rare in complex models. Approximate methods are required.

docs.title: 20.2 MCMC Basics
docs.text:
Markov Chain Monte Carlo draws samples from posterior.

Common methods: - Metropolis-Hastings - Gibbs sampling - Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) - NUTS (adaptive HMC)

docs.title: 20.3 Variational Inference (VI)
docs.text:
VI converts inference to optimization by fitting a tractable distribution to posterior.

Tradeoff: - Faster than MCMC in large systems - May underestimate posterior uncertainty

docs.title: 20.4 Diagnostics
docs.text: Mandatory checks: - Trace plots - Effective sample size - R-hat convergence metric - Posterior predictive checks

docs.title: 20.5 Prior Sensitivity Analysis
docs.text: Evaluate how conclusions change under alternative reasonable priors.

docs.title: 20.6 Hierarchical Bayesian Models
docs.text: Useful for multi-group AI systems (regions, products, segments) with partial pooling.

docs.title: 20.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Bayesian conversion model: - Update posteriors daily - Produce credible intervals by segment - Escalate when posterior probability of decline exceeds threshold

docs.title: 20.8 Deployment Guidance
docs.text:
• Cache posterior summaries
  • Monitor inference runtime
  • Use VI for low-latency approximations with periodic MCMC calibration

docs.title: 21 - Advanced Time Series and State-Space Models
21.1 Classical Models
docs.text:
• AR, MA, ARMA
  • ARIMA and seasonal SARIMA

Choose model family based on stationarity and seasonality diagnostics.

docs.title: 21.2 State-Space Models
docs.text:
Represent latent process and observation equations.

Core algorithms: - Kalman filter (online estimation) - Kalman smoother (retrospective refinement)

docs.title: 21.3 Volatility Modeling
docs.text:
• ARCH/GARCH for conditional variance dynamics
  • Useful in risk-sensitive forecasting

docs.title: 21.4 Change-Point Detection
docs.text: Detect regime changes in mean/variance/structure. Critical for production monitoring.

docs.title: 21.5 Multivariate Time Series
docs.text:
• VAR models
  • Dynamic factor models
  • Granger-causality intuition (predictive, not causal proof)

docs.title: 21.6 Forecast Uncertainty
docs.text: Always produce prediction intervals, not only point forecasts.

docs.title: 21.7 Backtesting and Validation
docs.text:
• Rolling-origin evaluation
  • Time-respecting splits
  • Forecast horizon-specific metrics

docs.title: 21.8 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Forecast API traffic by minute: - Use state-space model - Trigger autoscaling from upper prediction interval - Log interval coverage drift weekly

docs.title: 22.1 Event-Time Data
docs.text: Outcome is time until event (churn, failure, conversion), often with censoring.

docs.title: 22.2 Censoring Types
docs.text:
• Right censoring (most common)
  • Left censoring
  • Interval censoring

Ignoring censoring biases naive estimates.

docs.title: 22.3 Survival and Hazard Functions
docs.text:
• Survival: S(t) = P(T > t)
  • Hazard: instantaneous event rate at time t

docs.title: 22.4 Kaplan-Meier Estimator
docs.text: Nonparametric estimator of survival curve with censoring.

docs.title: 22.5 Cox Proportional Hazards Model
docs.text: Semi-parametric model: - interpretable hazard ratios - proportional hazards assumption needs checking

docs.title: 22.6 Competing Risks
docs.text: When multiple event types can terminate process, use competing-risk analysis.

docs.title: 22.7 Calibration and Utility
docs.text: For churn-risk interventions, calibrate predicted survival probabilities and optimize intervention timing.

docs.title: 22.8 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Subscription churn system: - Estimate survival curve by cohort - Trigger retention offers where hazard peaks - Evaluate lift with controlled experiments

docs.title: 23.1 Potential Outcomes Framework
docs.text:
• Y(1) outcome under treatment
  • Y(0) outcome under control
  • Individual treatment effect is unobservable jointly

Targets: - ATE (average treatment effect) - ATT (effect on treated)

docs.title: 23.2 Identification Assumptions
docs.text:
• Consistency
  • Positivity
  • Exchangeability / no unmeasured confounding (for observational designs)

docs.title: 23.3 Propensity Score Methods
docs.text:
• Matching
  • Stratification
  • Inverse probability weighting
  • Overlap diagnostics required

docs.title: 23.4 Doubly Robust Estimation
docs.text: Combines outcome model and propensity model; consistent if one is correctly specified.

docs.title: 23.5 Instrumental Variables
docs.text: Use when unmeasured confounding exists and valid instrument is available.

docs.title: 23.6 Difference-in-Differences
docs.text: Estimate treatment effect from pre/post changes in treated vs control. Requires parallel-trends assessment.

docs.title: 23.7 Regression Discontinuity
docs.text: Exploit cutoff-based treatment assignment for local causal effects.

docs.title: 23.8 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Policy change in recommender ranking: - Estimate causal impact on retention - Run observational doubly robust estimate - Validate with randomized holdout where feasible

docs.title: 24 - Advanced Experimental Design and Sequential Testing
24.1 Beyond Basic A/B Testing
docs.text: Production experimentation often includes repeated looks, multiple metrics, and operational constraints.

docs.title: 24.2 Sequential and Group-Sequential Testing
docs.text:
Allow interim analyses while controlling type-I error.

Tools: - Alpha-spending functions - O'Brien-Fleming and Pocock-style boundaries

docs.title: 24.3 CUPED and Variance Reduction
docs.text: Use pre-experiment covariates to reduce variance and shorten test duration.

docs.title: 24.4 Multi-Armed Bandits
docs.text: Adaptive allocation methods improve cumulative reward but complicate unbiased effect estimation.

docs.title: 24.5 Interference and Network Effects
docs.text: When units affect each other, standard independence assumptions fail. Use cluster-based designs where possible.

docs.title: 24.6 Cluster and Switchback Designs
docs.text:
• Cluster randomized trials for spillover contexts
  • Switchback tests for marketplace/logistics systems

docs.title: 24.7 Metric Governance
docs.text: Define: - Primary metric - Guardrail metrics - Kill-switch rules - Pre-registered analysis plan

docs.title: 24.8 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Ranking experiment in marketplace: - Switchback by hour/region - Guardrail on cancellation rate - Sequential stopping with controlled false-positive risk

docs.title: 25.1 High-Dimensional Regime
docs.text: When features p are comparable to or greater than samples n, classical estimators become unstable.

docs.title: 25.2 Regularization Strategies
docs.text:
• Ridge (L2): shrink coefficients smoothly
  • Lasso (L1): sparse feature selection
  • Elastic Net: balance sparsity and stability

docs.title: 25.3 Feature Selection Stability
docs.text: Selection can vary strongly across samples. Use stability selection and repeated resampling.

docs.title: 25.4 Sparse Covariance and Graphical Models
docs.text: Estimate sparse precision matrices for conditional dependency structure.

docs.title: 25.5 Multiple Testing in High Dimensions
docs.text: Control false discoveries with: - Benjamini-Hochberg FDR - Knockoff-style methods

docs.title: 25.6 Dimension Reduction
docs.text:
• PCA and sparse PCA
  • Autoencoder embeddings (statistical caution on interpretability)

docs.title: 25.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Text-based risk model with 100k sparse features: - train elastic-net logistic model - monitor selected-feature drift across retrains

docs.title: 25.8 Practical Guardrails
docs.text:
• Standardize features
  • Validate under repeated folds
  • Report confidence/stability for selected features

docs.title: 26.1 Empirical Risk Minimization
docs.text: Models optimize training loss, but objective is low expected risk on unseen data.

docs.title: 26.2 Bias-Variance Decomposition
docs.text: Generalization error combines approximation bias and estimation variance.

docs.title: 26.3 Capacity and Complexity
docs.text:
Model capacity concepts: - VC dimension intuition - Rademacher complexity intuition

Higher capacity increases overfitting risk without enough data or regularization.

docs.title: 26.4 Generalization Bounds (Conceptual)
docs.text: PAC-style guarantees provide probabilistic upper bounds on test risk given complexity and sample size.

docs.title: 26.5 Domain Shift and OOD
docs.text: Training and deployment distributions may differ. Track: - covariate shift - label shift - concept shift

docs.title: 26.6 Validation Design
docs.text:
• Nested CV for model selection fairness
  • Time-aware splits for sequential data
  • Segment-aware validation for reliability

docs.title: 26.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Model retains offline AUC but fails in new geography due to covariate shift. Mitigation: region-aware calibration + monitoring + targeted retraining.

docs.title: 27.1 Types of Uncertainty
docs.text:
• Aleatoric: inherent data noise
  • Epistemic: uncertainty from limited knowledge/data

docs.title: 27.2 Predictive Intervals
docs.text: For regression or forecasts, provide interval estimates with target coverage.

docs.title: 27.3 Probability Calibration
docs.text:
Classification probabilities should match observed frequencies.

Methods: - Temperature scaling - Platt scaling - Isotonic regression

docs.title: 27.4 Calibration Diagnostics
docs.text:
• Reliability diagrams
  • Expected calibration error (ECE)
  • Brier score

docs.title: 27.5 Conformal Prediction
docs.text:
Distribution-free uncertainty sets with finite-sample coverage guarantees (under assumptions).

Variants: - Split conformal - Cross-conformal - Mondrian conformal (group-conditional)

docs.title: 27.6 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Medical triage model: - output risk score + conformal prediction set - defer uncertain cases to clinicians - monitor empirical coverage by subgroup

docs.title: 27.7 Deployment Guidance
docs.text:
• Recalibrate after significant drift
  • Track interval width and coverage together
  • Use selective prediction/defer policies in high-risk systems

docs.title: 28.1 Why Robust Methods
docs.text: Real-world AI data often contains outliers, heavy tails, and labeling noise.

docs.title: 28.2 Robust Location and Scale
docs.text:
• Median and MAD
  • Trimmed means
  • Winsorization

docs.title: 28.3 Robust Estimation Frameworks
docs.text:
• Huber loss
  • Tukey biweight
  • M-estimators

docs.title: 28.4 Influence and Breakdown
docs.text:
• Influence function measures estimator sensitivity
  • Breakdown point quantifies robustness under contamination

docs.title: 28.5 Anomaly Detection Methods
docs.text:
• Statistical z-score and robust z-score
  • Isolation Forest
  • One-class SVM
  • Local Outlier Factor
  • Extreme Value Theory thresholding

docs.title: 28.6 Contamination Modeling
docs.text: Explicitly model expected contamination fraction for stable thresholds.

docs.title: 28.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Fraud pipeline: - robustly estimate transaction baseline - detect anomalies with EVT-calibrated threshold - update thresholds by region and time-of-day

docs.title: 28.8 Operational Rules
docs.text:
• Separate anomaly detection from final decision policy
  • Track false-positive burden on review teams
  • Recompute baselines periodically

docs.title: 29 - Fairness, Privacy, and Responsible Statistical AI
29.1 Fairness Metrics
docs.text:
Common group-level metrics: - Demographic parity - Equal opportunity - Equalized odds - Calibration within groups

Metrics can conflict; selection depends on context, harm model, and policy.

docs.title: 29.2 Counterfactual Fairness Intuition
docs.text: Assess whether prediction would remain similar under counterfactual changes to protected attributes.

docs.title: 29.3 Bias Diagnostics
docs.text:
• Performance by subgroup
  • Error asymmetry analysis
  • Threshold impact analysis

docs.title: 29.4 Privacy-Preserving Statistics
docs.text:
• Differential privacy basics (epsilon, delta)
  • Noise mechanisms for aggregate statistics
  • Membership-inference risk awareness

docs.title: 29.5 Governance Practices
docs.text:
• Model cards and data cards
  • Decision logs for high-impact thresholds
  • Human override and escalation paths

docs.title: 29.6 Compliance-Oriented Reporting
docs.text: Every deployment report should include: - fairness metrics by segment - uncertainty and confidence reporting - privacy risk notes - approved mitigations

docs.title: 29.7 Real-Time Example
docs.text: Loan-risk model: - optimize recall subject to fairness constraints - calibrate probabilities separately by allowed policy framework - require manual review for high-uncertainty borderline decisions

docs.title: 30 - Advanced Capstone, Research Playbooks, and Mastery Checklist
30.1 Advanced Capstone Tracks
docs.text: Choose one: 1. Causal uplift modeling for intervention allocation 2. Bayesian hierarchical forecasting system 3. Uncertainty-calibrated classification with conformal sets 4. High-dimensional sparse modeling with stability guarantees 5. Sequential experimentation platform with guardrails

docs.title: 30.2 Required Deliverables
docs.text:
• Problem framing and assumptions
  • Statistical method selection rationale
  • Reproducible code and experiment logs
  • Uncertainty-aware results
  • Robustness and fairness analysis
  • Deployment and monitoring plan

docs.title: 30.3 Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
• Methodologically valid design
  • Clear uncertainty quantification
  • Strong holdout/generalization performance
  • No unresolved critical leakage/bias issues
  • Incident and rollback plan documented

docs.title: 30.4 Research Playbook Template
docs.text:
Question
Data and assumptions
Method and identification strategy
Diagnostics and robustness checks
Results with uncertainty
Limitations and risks
Decision recommendation

docs.title: 30.5 Advanced Mastery Checklist
docs.text:
• Can diagnose when frequentist vs Bayesian is appropriate
  • Can design valid experiments under operational constraints
  • Can quantify and communicate uncertainty for stakeholders
  • Can apply causal reasoning beyond correlation
  • Can detect and mitigate drift, bias, and robustness failures
  • Can design production-ready statistical governance

docs.title: 30.6 Next-Level Study Roadmap
docs.text:
• Probabilistic graphical models
  • Causal machine learning
  • Advanced Monte Carlo methods
  • Statistical decision theory
  • Domain-specific regulation-aware evaluation

docs.title: Statistical Foundations for AI - Index
docs.text: This course uses one index file with two sections.
